questions.prompt: What does a Git commit actually contain?

choices.text: The deltas (changes) since the previous commit.

A complete SNAPSHOT of every file at that point in time (as a tree), pointers to its parent commit(s), and metadata (author, timestamp, message). Git stores snapshots, not deltas — same content shares storage via content addressing (identical files = same blob hash). The commit's SHA-1 hash IS its ID, computed from content; change anything and the hash changes, meaning commits are immutable. You can't edit a commit; amending creates a new commit with a new hash. This determinism is what makes the DAG (directed acyclic graph) consistent and Git operations safe.

questions.explanation: Snapshot + parent pointers + metadata. Content-addressed; immutable. Storage is deduplicated.

questions.prompt: You pushed a commit to a shared `main` branch, then realized it had a bad message. You amended locally and tried to push again. What's the right approach?

choices.text: Don't rewrite shared history. Amending creates a NEW commit with a different hash; the original commit you pushed is still in everyone else's local repos. Force-pushing replaces remote history but creates conflicts for everyone else when they pull. Right approach: leave the commit as-is and live with the message, OR add a follow-up commit that explains/corrects (e.g., 'Note: previous commit message should have said X'). Force-pushing is only safe on branches you're sure no one else has pulled — typically your own feature branches before merging. The rule: rewrite history only on commits that exist only in your local branch.

questions.explanation: Never rewrite shared history. Force-push only on your own unshared branches.
